does not block. It keeps a priority-1 task ready on Core 0 forever. `yield()` lets equal/higher-priority ready tasks run, but it does not sleep this task or give lower-priority/idle work a clean window. That conflicts with the dual-core note that app code pinned to Core 0 should be minimal and only there for low-latency audio/radio-adjacent work, with no general-purpose loop parked there.
3. [src/main.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:9): this creates a second Arduino-loop-shaped task on Core 0, but there is no work ownership model yet.

Per [dual-core-load-distribution.md](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/dual-core-load-distribution.md:51), Core 0 app tasks should be a small audio pump if they exist at all. An empty always-running task reserves scheduler attention without serving the audio path. Better shape would be a task that blocks on a queue/notification/timer until there is audio work, or no Core 0 app task until the pump exists.

If task creation fails, `loopTask_core0_Handle` remains null and the firmware continues under a false “core 0 task exists” assumption. Low severity right now, but important once this owns real audio work.

The intent lines up with the doc’s broad split: Arduino `loop()` on Core 1, possible low-latency work on Core 0. The current implementation is still just a scaffold, and the main risk is that the Core 0 scaffold spins instead of blocking. Once the syntax error is fixed, I’d make the Core 0 task sleep/block until it has real audio pump work.

user_prompt: so `void loopTask(void *pvParameters)`, under some configurations, will never ever call taskYIELD?
agent_reply: Yes, in this `main.cpp`, `loopTask()` itself never directly calls `taskYIELD()`.
- **UNICORE build:** it may call `vTaskDelay(5)` every ~2 seconds via `yieldIfNecessary()`.
- **Dual-core build:** that block is compiled out, so `loopTask()` has no built-in yield/delay at all.

But that does **not** mean the task can never be preempted. FreeRTOS can still preempt it on tick interrupts if `CONFIG_FREERTOS_HZ` / preemption are enabled, and calls inside your `loop()` may block/yield/delay. It just means Arduino’s wrapper loop does not voluntarily yield for you on dual-core ESP32.
So if your `loop()` is a tight CPU-bound loop on dual core, it can keep its core busy indefinitely unless your code calls `delay()`, `vTaskDelay()`, `taskYIELD()`, blocks on I/O/queues, etc.

user_prompt: src/main.cpp:13:24: warning: ignoring attribute 'section (".rtc.data.2")' because it conflicts with previous 'section (".rtc.data.0")' [-Wattributes]
src/main.cpp:14:24: warning: ignoring attribute 'section (".rtc.data.3")' because it conflicts with previous 'section (".rtc.data.1")' [-Wattributes]
can you explain what the warnings mean? I was told that RTC_DATA_ATTR makes it store it in a slow persistent RAM region that is kept between resets if power is not lost
agent_reply: Yes, your understanding of `RTC_DATA_ATTR` is basically right: on ESP32 it places the variable into RTC memory, which can survive many reset types as long as power is not removed.
The warning is about applying `RTC_DATA_ATTR` in two places:

`RTC_DATA_ATTR` expands to a GCC section attribute, something like “put this symbol in `.rtc.data...`”. GCC is seeing one section attribute on the `extern` declaration and then another section attribute on the actual definition. Because ESP’s macro expansion gives them distinct internal section names, GCC says: “these declarations disagree, so I’m ignoring one of the attributes.”

The important practical bit: the variables are still likely ending up in RTC memory because the actual definitions in `main.cpp` have `RTC_DATA_ATTR`. But the header declarations should not carry that attribute. The clean pattern is:
So the warning does not mean RTC RAM does not work. It means the attribute was redundantly attached to both the declaration and definition, and GCC is complaining about the mismatch.

user_prompt: does M5 stuff need some sort of poll? Remember when we had problems using the Vbus signal for charging? does that call only read from a cache and need a poll to update?
agent_reply: Yes: M5 as a whole needs polling via `M5.update()`, especially for buttons/touch. In this project that already happens inside `FlyGui::poll()`, and `main.cpp` calls `gui->poll()` in the main loop.

For the charging/VBUS question specifically: `M5.Power.isCharging()` does **not** look like a cached value that only updates after `M5.update()`. In M5Unified it routes into the PMIC-specific implementation, like AXP192/AXP2101/IP5306, and reads the power chip state directly.
The cache to watch out for is **our** `BattTracker`: it reads M5 power state in `BattTracker::poll()`, but I don’t see `BattTracker::poll()` being called anywhere right now. So:
- `BattTracker::isCharging()` / `BattTracker::status()` may be stale after init unless `BattTracker::poll()` runs.
- The current FlyGui top bar bypasses `BattTracker` and calls `M5.Power.getBatteryLevel()` plus `M5.Power.isCharging()` directly.

So if the charging icon is wrong, I would not blame missing `M5.update()` first. More likely suspects are `isCharging()` vs “USB/VBUS present” semantics, top-bar redraw throttling/state caching, or some code path using stale `BattTracker` values.

agent_reply: That reboot makes sense now. The likely culprit was our pairing-complete path doing too much inside `ESP_BT_GAP_AUTH_CMPL_EVT`.

Specifically, the GAP callback was calling `g_host_list.insert()`, and that calls `BtManager::isBonded()`, which queries the GAP bonded-device list. That is re-entering Bluetooth/GAP from inside a Bluetooth/GAP callback, right at authentication completion. Very plausible RTC watchdog stall.
- GAP auth callback now only copies the paired device data and sets a pending flag.
- `BtManager::poll()` handles the Bluetooth-side post-pair work later: insert into BtManager’s internal list, fire paired callback, close pairing window, return state to idle.
- `main.cpp` now registers a paired callback and defers app-side work to the main loop: insert into the JSON-backed `bt_host_list`, save `bluetooth.json`, and return from `ConnWaitingView`.
This should stop the watchdog reset and also make a successful pair persist into the UI’s host list.

agent_reply: I think the culprit was our newer pairing-success behavior.

After pairing, we were briefly forcing Bluetooth to **non-connectable / non-discoverable** while the thumbs-up modal was shown. That was meant to be tidy, but Android appears to still be doing service discovery/profile setup after the bond completes. Cutting off connectability there can cause Android to cache the device with the HFP “Calls” profile disabled.
I changed pairing completion to keep Bluetooth **connectable / non-discoverable** instead. That matches the intended normal mode and gives Android room to finish enabling the Calls profile.
For testing, I’d forget/unpair this device on Android and pair fresh after flashing this. Android may have already cached the bad profile state from the previous behavior.

- It marks activity and lets Hotel restore full-speed mode before starting HFP.
- I removed the redundant `setConnectableNonDiscoverable()` call from the pairing success dialog path.

user_prompt: We have made a lot of progress, a lot of things are working the way I want
Walk through the documentation I wrote earlier, we might've deviated a lot. Warn me where the implementation does not match the written documentation, and tell me suggested changes
Note: I am thinking about making the device way more secure, and the `bluetooth.json` and `wifi.json` will eventually go away, so ignore things that involve that
agent_reply: 1. **The documented file-transfer/filesystem design no longer matches the code.**

The docs still say Wi-Fi provides an FTP server, the app treats onboard flash and microSD as separate disks, and LittleFS is used: [README.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/README.md:7), [file-system-and-ftp-server-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/file-system-and-ftp-server-design.md:1).
- no FTP implementation beyond a readme in [lib/FtpServer/readme.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FtpServer/readme.md:1)
- a microSD-only HTTP file server in [WebServer.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:376)
- that HTTP server is only visibly initialized by the webserver test, not normal firmware startup: [test_webserver.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_webserver.cpp:53)
Suggested change: decide the real transfer direction now.
- If HTTP/web UI is the intended path, update README and filesystem docs from “FTP + LittleFS + two disks” to “microSD file access over HTTP/web UI” and delete or mark FTP notes as obsolete.
- If FTP and onboard flash still matter, they are implementation gaps, not documentation details.
2. **Several documented Wi-Fi and UI workflows are still scaffolding.**

The docs describe AP mode, station selection, NTP sync, cloud upload selection/progress, file viewing, and RTC setting: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:3), [wifi-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/wifi-workflow.md:9).
- selecting a configured station, AP, cloud upload, and NTP action only logs: [main.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:530)
That would avoid the docs reading like finished product behavior while these surfaces are still stubs.
3. **The call-recording UI behavior has drifted from the written UI contract.**

The docs say the user can change call volume, mute/unmute the mic, and see a 3-pixel tally border that becomes dotted when local mic is muted: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:7). The Bluetooth workflow also says the recording screen shows mute status and output volume: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:19).
- exposes push-to-talk mode switching and **speaker** mute in [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:185)

- uses a fixed 10-pixel solid red frame in [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:21) and [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:403)
- has mic mute support in `AudioManager`, but I did not find GUI wiring for it: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1307)
- has volume APIs, but not a recording-view volume control: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1231)
Suggested change: choose one UI model.
- If the device is primarily push-to-talk on internal hardware, rewrite the docs around P2T and speaker mute.
- If mic mute and volume are still required call controls, add those controls and make the tally border encode mic state as documented.
4. **Recorder performance/file-format docs are slightly ahead of current behavior.**

The file-format doc says new recordings should pre-allocate roughly 1 GB and truncate afterward: [recording-file-format.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/recording-file-format.md:7). The dual-core note also assumes pre-grown `.rec` files: [dual-core-load-distribution.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/dual-core-load-distribution.md:113).

Current code has preallocation implemented but disabled by default: [conf.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:14), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:418).

Also, the doc says packets have a header and payload; the current on-disk implementation writes a **fixed-size** `file_packet_t` for normal `.rec` packets, including a fixed payload array and `payload_length`: [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:31), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:230).
- update the format doc to explicitly say `.rec` packets are fixed-size today
- say preallocation is optional/compile-time unless you intend to enable it again
5. **The high-level audio/hardware docs overstate two areas.**

The high-level design says an external WM8960 or SGTL5000 codec may be enabled by build flag: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:9). In the current audio manager, external I2S codec enable functions are explicit “not implemented yet” stubs: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:626).

The same doc says CVSD local PCM is 8 kHz: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:31). The implementation accepts 8 kHz HFP, but `AudioFifo` upsamples 8 kHz input into its 16 kHz internal representation, and recorder packets are labeled `*_16KHZ_MONO`: [AudioFifo.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:90), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:315).
- state “external codec path planned, internal M5Stack audio path implemented”
- HFP CVSD ingress/egress can be 8 kHz
- recorder/internal FIFO normalizes to 16 kHz today

- Bluetooth connection auto-starts a call recording after entering a connected/audio-available state, matching the Bluetooth workflow: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:13), [main.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:166).

- The recording stop path can also disconnect Bluetooth: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:17), [RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp:64).

- Wi-Fi activation shuts down Bluetooth/audio/recording first, which matches the high-level design: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:17), [WifiManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:291).

- RTC-based recording naming and `.rec` output are in place: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:21), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:68).
- Top bar date/time and battery status are implemented: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:16), [FlyGui.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:630).

Ignoring the eventual removal of `bluetooth.json` and `wifi.json` as requested: the recording format already has security hooks, but they are placeholders right now. `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY` adds nonce fields and leaves encryption/authentication as TODOs: [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:39), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:206).
Before hardening transfer/config storage, I would write one short security design doc that states:
- how keys are provisioned and recovered
- what local UI actions are allowed without trust/authentication
- whether the HTTP file surface survives at all
I did not count `bluetooth.json` or `wifi.json` mismatches in the findings. I also did not run builds or tests for this pass; this was a documentation-to-code review.

first, does starting a memo recording stop bluetooth incoming connections?

if not, then would it be difficult to reconfigure the RecordingView for a Bluetooth upon incoming connection, and then force a redraw? Without interrupting the file write, and maintaining the mic mode but now allowing the user to start using the speaker?
come up with a plan if this is feasible, or, if this is already implemented, let me know
agent_reply: Short answer: **starting a memo recording does not itself stop Bluetooth**. The memo path calls `beginMemoRecording()`, which:
It does **not** call `BtManager::disconnect()`, `shutdown()`, or change Bluetooth scan/connectability.

Important catch: Bluetooth is currently initialized as **non-connectable / non-discoverable** in `BluetoothManager.cpp`, and `setConnectableNonDiscoverable()` exists but does not appear to be used in normal startup. So host-initiated “incoming” connections may already be disabled most of the time, but not because memo recording started.
This is **not already implemented**. In [main.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:244), `handle_pending_bluetooth_recording()` currently does this:
So if Bluetooth connects while memo recording is already active, the pending Bluetooth recording event is ignored. The view stays in memo mode.
1. Decide where Bluetooth should be connectable.
If you want incoming connections during memo, call `BtManager::setConnectableNonDiscoverable()` during normal idle/memo-capable operation, and avoid it during Wi-Fi/AP modes.
2. Add a “promote memo to Bluetooth UI” path.
Do not call `AudioFileRecorder::startRecording()` again. Just switch the existing `RecordingView` with `configureBluetoothMode()` and force redraw.
3. Unchoke mic-to-Bluetooth without restarting the file.

Memo mode currently chokes `micToBluetoothFifo()`. Expose a helper in `RecordingViewCallbacks` that calls the existing restore logic, keeps `AudioManager` in mic mode, and does not touch the recorder.
If recording is already active, promote the view and audio routing instead of starting a new recording.
5. Optional but smart: clear `AudioManager::bluetoothToSpeakerFifo()` when the user first switches to speaker, so old buffered call audio does not play late.

The file write should be fine: `AudioFileRecorder` already records both Bluetooth-to-file and mic-to-file FIFOs into the same open file. A memo recording can become a hybrid recording without closing/reopening the file. The main caveat is that the filename/type code would still be memo unless we deliberately add metadata for “memo became Bluetooth.”

user_prompt: read the flow diagram in `developer-docs\ui-design.md` and compare it against the current implementation, there will be things not implemented, and things maybe implemented differently
agent_reply: I compared `developer-docs/ui-design.md` against the current FlyGui/main flow. The short version: the broad skeleton exists, but the implementation has collapsed several diagram nodes into one reusable `ScrollView` plus `ModalDialog`, and the Cloud/Wi-Fi-connected path is much less complete than the diagram implies.
- Boot to Splash to Main exists. Splash stays up until touch: [SplashView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/SplashView.cpp:20>).
- Top bar is global in `FlyGui::drawTopBar`, with date/time, battery, and disk warning: [FlyGui.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:665>).
- Main screen has common actions: Bluetooth, info, memo, phone/laptop quick connect, Wi-Fi: [MainScreenView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/MainScreenView.cpp:32>).
- Recording has the thick red tally border: [RecordingView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:447>).
- Memo recording exists and can cycle memo type while recording: [RecordingView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:289>).
- Bluetooth connect/pair/info/delete are effectively implemented through the Bluetooth `ScrollView` context and dialogs.
- Wi-Fi AP mode exists as `WifiApModeView`, with credentials and live AP stats.
- Recording stop goes to a finished confirmation dialog, then Main: [RecordingView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:257>).

- `Bluetooth`, `WiFi`, and `Cloud` are not separate concrete screen classes. They are mostly contexts inside one `ScrollView`; the enum still has older-style placeholders like `FLYGUI_VIEW_BLUETOOTH`, `FLYGUI_VIEW_WIFI`, `FLYGUI_VIEW_WEB_ACTION`, etc., but those views are not registered in `init_gui`: [all_init.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:110>).
- `BTConnect` and `BTPair` are both represented by `ConnWaitingView` modes, not separate view classes: [all_init.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:180>).
- `InfoScreen`, `BTInfo`, `WiFiInfo`, `BTErrorDialog`, `WifiErrorDialog`, `CloudErrorDialog`, and finished confirmations are all `ModalDialog` with different text/icons/return views.
- `BTUnpairConfirm` is implemented as ScrollView delete mode plus a modal confirmation, returning to the same Bluetooth scroll context.

- Cloud upload completion always reboots after dismissing the completion/failure dialog, matching the diagram’s success path but also doing it for failure completion: [main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:877>).

- `WiFiConnect --> established --> Cloud` is not currently wired. `scanAndConnect()` starts scan/connect, but once connected there is no callback registered to populate/show the Cloud scroll context. `populateCloud()` exists, but I don’t see it called anywhere.
- Selecting an individual Wi-Fi station does nothing right now: `onclick_wifi_station` only logs: [main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:638>).
- `Cloud` as a user-visible menu is not reachable from the current flow, despite `ScrollView::populateCloud()` existing.
- `CloudEnroll` and `CloudEnrollConfirmation` are not implemented as GUI flows.
- `PasswordReset` and `MemoryReset` are web/AP administration features, not on-device GUI screens. The AP view intentionally has no stop/reset button except physical reset/power.
- `WiFiAP --> password reset/memory reset --> Boot` is therefore not a FlyGui transition.
- `BTConnect --> cancel --> Main` is slightly different: cancel returns to the remembered previous view, often the Bluetooth scroll, not always Main.
- `BTErrorDialog --> Main` is often `BTErrorDialog --> Scroll`, because connection errors return to the Bluetooth list.
- “Change volume during actual calls” does not appear implemented in the recording UI. There is mic/speaker mode and speaker mute behavior, but not volume adjustment.
- “Mute or unmute mic” is only partially aligned; the UI toggles audio mode and speaker mute, not a clear mic mute/unmute control.

Incoming Bluetooth connection to Recording is substantially implemented through pending flags and `on_bluetooth_state_changed`/recording handling. It applies broadly, with special suppression during pairing confirmation and with Wi-Fi AP activation shutting Bluetooth down, which matches the “except when Wi-Fi radio owns the radio” intent.

user_prompt: does the change you just made effect how the ESPAsyncWebServer and AsyncTCP works?
agent_reply: Yes, but probably not the way you’re most worried about.

`ESPAsyncWebServer` / `AsyncTCP` should still work at the TCP socket level because the new path still creates the ESP netif through `wifiLowLevelInit(false)`, applies `esp_wifi_set_mode(WIFI_MODE_AP)`, configures `WIFI_IF_AP`, then calls `esp_wifi_start()`. AsyncTCP listens through lwIP, not through `WiFi.softAP()` specifically.

The risky part is Arduino’s AP wrapper bookkeeping. Because we now bypass `WiFi.softAP()` / `WiFi.AP.begin()`, helpers like `WiFi.softAPIP()` may return `0.0.0.0` or stale data even though the AP itself is up. I already dodged that in the new log line, but I found existing uses here that may need the same treatment:

So: the web server should still accept connections by IP, but the UI/API may report the wrong AP IP unless we move those call sites to a lower-level AP IP helper too. Also, mDNS is the one “keep an eye on it” item, since it can be sensitive to interface/event setup.

user_prompt: is the page for index.html served at 192.168.4.1 supposed to be under http or https?

agent_reply: It is supposed to be **HTTP**, not HTTPS:

The ping/DHCP side working but the browser getting “connection refused” means the AP was up, but port 80 was not listening. The real UI AP path was starting SoftAP and showing `WifiApModeView`, but it was not calling `WebServer::init()`. The tests did call it manually, which is why this was easy to miss.

I fixed that in [src/main.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:21) and [src/main.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:682): after AP start succeeds, it now starts the web server before showing the AP view.
Build passed. Note: `pio run` regenerated `src/version.c`, but the actual functional change is in `src/main.cpp`.

agent_reply: That crash is not the serial port. It’s an interrupt watchdog in the Bluedroid HFP client on core 0 during service-level connection. Core 1 was in `AudioFileRecorder::pump()` trying to take the recorder mutex, even though there may be no active recording.
I made the recorder pump path much less intrusive:
- Switched the recorder active flag to an atomic fast check in [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:66).
- `pump()` now immediately returns when no recording is active.
- [main.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:345) now skips calling `pump()` entirely unless `needsPump()` is true.
This should stop the idle main loop from hammering FreeRTOS mutex/queue internals during Bluetooth pairing/HFP setup.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`: build succeeded. If the watchdog still fires on pairing, the next suspect is the temporary HFP debug build flags in `platformio.ini`.

user_prompt: the crash was upon completion of pairing
agent_reply: Yep, that explains the stack traces. Pairing completion was letting the phone immediately kick off HFP service setup, which is the fragile Bluedroid path.
I changed it so boot/pairing use Bluetooth GAP only, and HFP is initialized lazily when you actually connect to a saved host:
- Changed `setup()` in [main.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp) to use GAP-only init, while still storing audio callbacks.
- Changed `startPairing()` in [BluetoothManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BluetoothManager.cpp) to avoid HFP during pairing, and deinit HFP before pairing if it was already up.
- Left the internal HFP debug flags disabled in [platformio.ini](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini), since they’re still too close to the WDT path for comfort.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`, build passes.

user_prompt: it's working, kind of, device to host and host to device connections both work

pairing is a bad experience, I think it looked like it rebooted but I didn't see any messages at all in my terminal, the serial port is working, uh, maybe the confirmation dialog registered the wrong return view so it returned to the splash screen instead of the mainscreenview, make sure that's not possible

the press to hold for deleting pairing does work but there's no indication that the user held for long enough. I need the trash-can icon to show up as soon as the timespan for a long-hold has been reached, instead of being checked only on release.
it feels like I have to delete a pair twice before it disappears from the list.

I want you to do something different, you have prevented a connection after a pair, this makes the phone very unhappy. I want to go back to the way it was before, when we can always accept an incoming connection. We simply don't start a recording. I see `on_pairing_success_dialog_dismissed` already issues a reboot. That's good.
agent_reply: Implemented and verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`.
- Bluetooth now starts full HFP at boot again, so incoming host connections can be accepted normally.
- Pairing no longer deinitializes HFP or makes the radio non-connectable.
- Pairing mode suppresses auto-recording immediately, so a phone can connect after pairing without starting a recording.
- Pairing success dialog now falls back to `FLYGUI_VIEW_MAIN` before reboot, so it cannot land on splash if reboot is delayed or interrupted.
- Bluetooth long-hold delete now shows the trash icon as soon as the hold threshold is reached.
- Unpair no longer immediately saves through the path that re-reads bond state too early, which should fix the “delete twice before it disappears” behavior.
Build passes. One note: `pio` regenerated `src/version.c` during the build.

user_prompt: but the dismissal of the dialog goes back to the mainscreenview first and then reboots, so it looks more like a malfunction
agent_reply: Fixed the visual hop. Rebooting dialogs now keep the modal dialog on screen while the dismiss callback runs, instead of briefly showing `MainScreenView` first.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`, build passes. `src/version.c` was regenerated by the build again.

user_prompt: ok I put the missing sprites in, let's do this again
for the `ErrorView` I want a change
I want to place a button with `sprite_doorreturn_50` for non fatal errors, clicking it or `BtnC` is now what dismisses the dialog. Remove the bottom message.

For fatal errors, place a button with `sprite_firstaidwifi_50` and when clicking it, it does a best effort at launching the default secure soft AP and then showing the `WifiApModeView` . The polling loop for this will need to have `WifiManager` polling, and maybe `BattTracker::poll();` too.
Only show this new Wi-Fi button if `wifi_manager` is not null.

Make sure that the launching of the default secure softAP does not require the successful loading of NVS. I understand that a lot of the web functionality might fail, like `/get_info` or `/get_cfg`, make sure those fail gracefully with HTTP codes when NVS is not loaded. We do need the file uploader to work though.
agent_reply: - Nonfatal errors now show `sprite_doorreturn_50`; only that button or `BtnC` dismisses.
- Removed the bottom “touch screen” / fatal restart text.
- Fatal errors now show `sprite_firstaidwifi_50` when `wifi_manager` exists.
- The fatal Wi-Fi button best-effort starts the generated secure SoftAP, starts the web server, and switches to `WifiApModeView`.

agent_reply: Yes, the WebCrypto shim was genuinely broken for this AP use case.

The old `webcrypto-shim.min.js` expected runtime globals like `self.asmCrypto`, but the page only loaded the shim itself. On an HTTP soft AP where native `crypto.subtle` is unavailable, HMAC/AES-GCM then fell into missing shim internals, producing `n is not a constructor`.

I rebuilt [html/webcrypto-shim.min.js](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/html/webcrypto-shim.min.js>) with the crypto implementation bundled in, then regenerated [inc/web_assets.h](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/web_assets.h>) so firmware embeds the fixed page.

user_prompt: what is the difference between `show_error_dialog` and `show_fatal_error_f` with fatal set to false?
agent_reply: `show_fatal_error_f(false, ...)` is a non-fatal use of the dedicated full-screen `ErrorView`.
- Caller chooses the return view with `next_view`
- Returns immediately after showing the modal
- Caller often handles fallback if showing it fails
- Captures the current view, shows the error screen, and blocks/polls until dismissed
- Then returns to the previous view automatically
- With `fatal=false`, the button is a return/door icon, not emergency AP

So practically: use `show_error_dialog` for ordinary user-facing flow errors where the app should keep running naturally. Use `show_fatal_error_f(false, ...)` for “serious but recoverable” errors where you want a full-screen interruption, logging, formatting, and automatic return to where the user was.

user_prompt: the diagnostic mechanism is telling me that during a bluetooth call session with recording active, if my local mic is off, then the redraw rate is 60 FPS as expected as it is capped at 60, but when I use push-to-talk, the frame rate drops to 45 FPS
the diagnostics show that 16000 samples goes both ways in both circumstances, and I don't detect any FIFO overflows, and everything does seem to function correctly

but the frame rate dropping is weird, the audio processing, the AGC and HPF, those are all core0, while the GUI draws are core1. The file writing and encoding are done after 2 mono streams are mixed into stereo so it shouldn't matter if the mic is on or off
agent_reply: You’re probably missing that the recorder/encoder is on core1 with the GUI, not core0.

In [main.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:258), `gui->poll()` runs on core1, then [AudioFileRecorder::pump()](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:262) runs immediately after it on the same core. The recording view sets audio mode to a 60 Hz GUI poll cap in [RecordingView.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:163), and FPS is counted only when `gui->poll()` actually draws in [FlyGui.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:163). So if the recorder pump gets heavier, the GUI simply misses 60 Hz slots.

The key detail: MP3 compression is enabled in [conf.h](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:21). With mic off, the mic channel going to the recording is synthesized silence via `queue_silence_to_match()` in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:913). With push-to-talk, the mic channel is real signal and gets mixed into stereo, then encoded by `write_mp3_samples()` in [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:1771). Same 16k samples/sec, different encoder cost. Silence is cheap; live mic noise/speech is not.

There’s also a mode-routing change: PTT enters mic mode and chokes the speaker FIFO in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:856), so the recorder is pairing real mic FIFO data with BT host FIFO data rather than pairing BT host data with generated silence. That can change burstiness and mutex/FIFO pressure even when average sample rates look perfect.

I’d verify by timing `AudioFileRecorder::pump()` total duration and separately timing `write_mp3_samples()`. Your existing long-write stats only catch SD/encrypted write duration, not encoder time. Quick A/B: temporarily disable `BUILD_USE_MP3_COMPRESSION`; if PTT goes back to 60 FPS, that’s the smoking crater.

user_prompt: Write for me `lib\ExtCodec\ExtCodec.cpp` and its associated header file. Please follow the guidelines `developer-docs\code-layout-policy.md` as you write the code
There will be only one instance, make it a namespaced module, and it will work tightly with `AudioManager`
The codec is the `SGTL5000`, and I have added the files `lib\ExtCodec\control_sgtl5000.cpp` and `lib\ExtCodec\control_sgtl5000.h` to help this effort get started.

There will be a initialization function. This will first check the I2C address to see if it responds, and the rest of `AudioManager` and `RecordingView`behavior will depend on if it is available. This is to be done while the splash screen is still happening. Later we might even update the splash screen to indicate if an external CODEC is connected.
there should be a state enum like
GPIO35 is an ADC capable pin and is dedicated to sensing if a earbud is connected, a high-ish voltage means connected
GPIO36 is an ADC capable pin and is dedicated to sensing if a earbud has an inline mic, a low-ish voltage would indicate there’s no mic (as it will be shorted to HP-GND physically)

If the external codec is connected, then we will start up a dedicated ADC thread. It doesn’t need much stack. All it does is do ADC reads once every 100ms, blocking yet yielding. It sets the state of the module accordingly and maybe set an event flag. Other threads will use the state change to reconfigure electrically and reconfigure the GUI accordingly
Another mic is connected to line-in-right of the `SGTL5000` and it will be used if the earbud-inline-mic is not available
Remember that the `SGTL5000` will use stereo and a different BCLK pin for the I2S. The entire I2S does need to be reconfigured when we are switching between the SGTL5000 and the NS4168.
So in our current implementation, we are already operating under `EXTCODEC_UNAVAIL`. No change in behavior needed

Under state `EXTCODEC_NO_EARBUD` we still have push-to-talk behavior, but instead of switching to the `SPM1423` when we push-to-talk, we switch to the `SGTL5000` using line-in-right as the sound source.

Under `EXTCODEC_YES_EARBUD` we have bidirectional audio, the mic is using line-in-right, do not use push-to-talk behavior, we can mute the mic, we can mute the sound output, but now changing mic state doesn’t change sound output state, and changing sound output state doesn’t change mic state

Under `EXTCODEC_YES_EARBUD_WITH_MIC` we have bidirectional audio, the mic is using the `SGTL5000`'s dedicated mic input, do not use push-to-talk behavior, we can mute the mic, we can mute the sound output, but now changing mic state doesn’t change sound output state, and changing sound output state doesn’t change mic state

Add to `inc\pins.h`, the I2S_BCLK pin for the SGTL5000 will be GPIO13, the I2S_MCLK pin will be GPIO14 (and only used if the external codec is detected). GPIO34 is for the I2S data for the mic. GPIO2 is for the I2S data for the earbud. GPIO0 is for the I2S_LRCLK.
`AudioFifo` already has special enqueue and dequeue functions for handling stereo data, if these are missing then implement them
